does: This paper introduces an empirical red-teaming framework to measure the Overton Windows (OWs) of open-source LLMs—the range of political opinions they can reliably express on controversial topics. The authors evaluate over 30 LLMs across 10 model families from five countries, measuring how susceptible each model is to simple natural-language jailbreaks that expand their political expressivity. They systematically quantify political asymmetries in model outputs, finding that open-source LLMs exhibit systematic left-leaning biases, that OWs contract as model size increases, and that regional origins significantly influence political expression patterns despite uneven representation in the open-source landscape. The paper provides concrete measurements of these vulnerabilities rather than theoretical analysis alone.
connects: This work builds on a growing body of research into LLM safety, alignment, and bias detection that emerged as these models became widely deployed. It extends prior red-teaming methodologies developed for frontier API-only models by adapting them to locally-deployed open-source LLMs, which are the actual target of adversaries seeking to circumvent safety measures. The paper connects to literature on LLM jailbreaking and adversarial robustness while responding to concerns about AI-enabled disinformation and influence operations highlighted by AI safety researchers. It also engages with work on measuring political bias in language models and understanding how training procedures and regional differences shape model behavior.
enables: This framework enables future research into understanding how readily open-source LLMs can be weaponized for coordinated influence campaigns, providing benchmarks for red-teaming practitioners and AI safety teams. The identified vulnerabilities and systematic asymmetries point toward improved alignment techniques for open-source models and better detection methods for AI-generated political content. The work facilitates policy discussions around open-source model release decisions by quantifying specific operational risks. It also enables development of more robust guardrails and evaluation tools, while prompting investigation into why certain architectural and training choices lead to systematic political biases that jailbreaks can exploit.
